children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

31.I.1	The International Civil Service Commission (ICSC) was established pursuant to General Assembly resolution 3357 (XXIX) and is responsible for the regulation and coordination of the conditions of service for the United Nations common system. Under its statute, the Commission is a subsidiary organ of the Assembly. In the exercise of its functions, the Commission is guided by the principles set out in the agreements between the United Nations and other organizations aimed at the development of a single unified international civil service through the application of common personnel standards, methods and arrangements.
Programme of work
Objective
31.I.2	The objective, to which ICSC contributes, is to ensure fair and harmonized conditions of service for staff in the United Nations common system.
Strategy and external factors for 2027
31.I.3	To contribute to the objective, the Commission will:
(a)	Continue to develop and strengthen a common system of salaries, allowances and benefits under the Noblemaire and Flemming principles;
(b)	Apply the methodologies for determining salaries and post adjustment classifications;
(c)	Conduct salary surveys for the General Service and related categories;
(d)	Provide guidance and advice on the administration of human resources management policies and systems, including job evaluation standards and tools, for all stakeholders;
(e)	Lend its expertise to stakeholders and work closely with the Human Resources Network of the United Nations System Chief Executives Board for Coordination (CEB);
(f)	Seek feedback from organizations on the conditions of service of staff;
(g)	Conduct workshops to educate staff on the work of the Commission and on the benefits and entitlements available to staff of the United Nations common system;
(h)	Monitor and report to the General Assembly on the implementation of its decisions by common system organizations, and recommend appropriate measures to ensure compliance;
(i)	Analyse the conditions of service of staff in difficult duty stations;
(j)	Periodically conduct comprehensive reviews of the common system compensation package;
(k)	Explore options for non-financial incentives for staff of the common system.

31.I.4	In an effort to increase diversity and rejuvenate the workforce within the common system, the Commission will continue monitoring trends in the common system organizations and propose relevant updates to the General Assembly on any new developments as they relate to geographical diversity and the rejuvenation of the workforce.
31.I.5	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	The promotion of geographical diversity and rejuvenation of the workforce;
(b)	A more coherent, attractive and effective common-system human resources management system that is aligned closely with the achievement of organizational goals and objectives;
(c)	Adequate conditions of service for all categories of staff.

31.I.6	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the planning assumption that organizations of the United Nations common system provide timely information, as requested by the Commission, and that they fully implement the Commission’s decisions and recommendations.

31.I.7	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, the Commission will continue to conduct reference checks with other international organizations to ascertain whether the organizations of the common system remain competitive and continue to be attractive on the labour market.

31.I.8	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, within the United Nations common system, the Commission will continue to engage with staff and heads of common system organizations through various forums. Engaging with staff and management of the various organizations will strengthen existing partnerships and lead to greater cooperation among stakeholders and full implementation of decisions and recommendations of the Commission and the General Assembly. This will also contribute to the timely completion of reviews requested of the Commission.

31.I.9	The Commission integrates a gender perspective in its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate, and will support efforts aimed at promoting gender equality in the workplace.
31.I.10	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Commission will continue its efforts to support staff and their dependants with disabilities.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative
31.I.11	The Commission will be guided by any future decisions of the General Assembly in the context of the UN80 initiative, as appropriate and as mandated.
Programme performance in 2025
A fair and equitable personal transitional allowance model

31.I.12	The personal transitional allowance was first introduced in 1992 to mitigate sudden decreases in the post adjustment multiplier upon the implementation of negative survey results. While the existing model had functioned adequately in most duty stations, the Commission identified shortcomings in cases involving exceptionally large personal transitional allowances, which resulted in prolonged payment periods and disparities between staff receiving the allowance and those who were not, creating tensions based on fairness and equity. For example, a personal transitional allowance promulgated in 2023 for Lebanon was projected to last until 2028. To address this, the Commission formed a focus group on extremely large personal transitional allowances to examine the issue and propose options to strengthen fairness and equity. The Commission considered the findings of the focus group and the application of revised parameters to existing allowances.
31.I.13	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 31.I.1).
Table 31.I.1
Performance measure

table_content.text: A focus group on extremely large personal transitional allowances made recommendations to the Commission

The Commission decided to modify any future personal transitional allowances by superimposing a maximum duration of 36 months and determined to further lower the maximum duration to 24 months for any new personal transitional allowances, in addition to revising the parameters regulating the allowances to further constrain their duration.

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: more flexibility afforded to staff working in extreme hardship locations – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

31.I.14	The Commission’s work contributed to the consideration by the General Assembly of an update on progress made regarding the comprehensive review of the compensation package, as reflected in the report of the International Civil Service Commission for 2025 (A/80/30 and A/80/30/Corr.1), which did not meet the planned target of approval by the General Assembly of the Commission’s recommendation to regularize the payment of an amount in lieu of a settling-in grant at category D and E duty stations that are not designated as non-family. The General Assembly decided, in its resolution 79/252 A-B, to continue the pilot programme for the payment of an amount in lieu of a settling-in grant at category D and E duty stations that are not designated as non-family until the conclusion of the comprehensive review of the compensation package.
31.I.15	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see table 31.I.2).

children.text: Result 2: revised standards of conduct to guide organizations on emerging issues – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

31.I.16	The Commission’s work contributed to the approval by the General Assembly of the revised standards of conduct for the international civil service, which met the planned target. Additionally, the General Assembly requested the Commission to incorporate comprehensive guidance, including on preventing conflicts of interest, promoting a culture of efficiency and the economical, responsible and efficient use of resources, as well as strengthening financial discipline and oversight, and to report thereon to the General Assembly in its next report.

children.text: Result 3: a compensation package that reflects the objectives, criteria and parameters approved by the General Assembly – Proposed programme plan for 2027

31.I.18	In its resolution 76/240, the General Assembly invited the Commission to undertake a comprehensive assessment and review of the compensation package for the United Nations common system on a five-year cycle. The review, which is due to be completed in 2026, focuses on the Professional and higher categories, specifically the allowances and benefits, as well as on new developments that have taken place since the previous review.
Lessons learned and planned change

31.I.19	The lesson for the Commission was that the ability to draw more conclusive evidence on the effectiveness of changes to the common system compensation package would benefit from improved granularity and comparability of data across the United Nations common system. In applying the lesson, the Commission will work with the organizations of the United Nations common system, in the context of the comprehensive review of the compensation package and beyond, to gradually enhance the granularity and comparability of the comprehensive data related to compensation costs and relevant expenditure. Notwithstanding these efforts, it is important to note that assessments are more conclusive when changes are afforded sufficient time to be fully implemented and reflected in the data.

children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

31.II.1	In line with article 5 of its Statute, approved by the General Assembly in its resolution 31/192, the Joint Inspection Unit is mandated to review all matters having a bearing on the efficiency of the services and the proper use of funds in the organizations of the United Nations system and to provide an independent view through inspection and evaluation aimed at improving management and methods and at achieving greater coordination between organizations. The Unit is the only external independent oversight body of the United Nations system that performs system-wide and single-organization assessments which identify areas for improvement and support the sharing of best practices among all the organizations that have adopted its statute. Effective 1 January 2027, the Unit’s oversight portfolio will expand, with the International Organization for Migration joining as the twenty-ninth participating organization.
Programme of work
Objective
31.II.2	The overarching objective, to which the Unit contributes, is to enhance the efficiency, effectiveness and coordination of the United Nations system.
Strategy and external factors for 2027
31.II.3	To contribute to the objective, the Unit will:

(a)	Ensure that its programme of work includes system-wide reviews covering one or more of the following thematic areas: (i) accountability and oversight functions and systems; (ii) management and administration practices; (iii) the implementation of policies, strategies and programmes; (iv) internationally agreed goals and conventions; and (v) governance arrangements and mechanisms, as well as inter-agency coordination;

(b)	Undertake reviews of management and administration practices of single organizations in the areas of governance, strategic planning, risk management, human resources, financial management, administration, information systems and oversight;
(c)	Review system-wide issues across all or multiple organizations, with an emphasis on efficiency and effectiveness and the management and implementation of the UN80 Initiative;

(d)	Analyse the acceptance and implementation of the Unit’s recommendations to identify how acceptance and implementation rates can be improved, including through a more effective consideration of the Unit’s reports by legislative organs and by executive heads.
31.II.4	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Better information for decision-making by the legislative organs, governing bodies and executive heads;
(b)	Enhanced efficiency and effectiveness of the organizations of the United Nations system;
(c)	Enhanced coordination and collaboration between organizations of the United Nations system.
31.II.5	With regard to external factors, the programme plan for 2027 is based on the planning assumption that participating organizations will continue to fully cooperate with the Unit’s reviews.

31.II.6	With regard to coordination and cooperation within the United Nations system, the Unit will continue to engage with key oversight bodies such as the Board of Auditors, the Office of Internal Oversight Services and oversight committees of participating organizations. The Unit will also maintain existing working relationships with internal oversight entities in participating organizations while pursuing new ones to foster cooperation.

31.II.7	The Unit integrates a gender perspective in its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. This may include examination and reporting on the gender policies and practices of organizations reviewed.

31.II.8	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Unit will take into account, where applicable, disability inclusion when planning and conducting reviews, with a view to helping to accelerate the effective mainstreaming of disability inclusion, including mitigating measures to overcome any challenges encountered in operationalizing disability inclusion.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

31.II.9	The Unit will consider the ongoing UN80 Initiative as an important input to the selection of topics for inclusion in its programme of work for 2027, which is expected to be approved in January 2027.
Programme performance in 2025
Increased responsiveness to Joint Inspection Unit recommendations through access to a new recommendation tracking tool

31.II.10	In 2025, the Unit replaced its recommendation tracking system with a new system developed in collaboration with the Office of Information and Communications Technology of the Secretariat of the United Nations. The new system enables the Unit and the organizations that are overseen by the Unit to have a better overview of the recommendations issued and of their acceptance and implementation status, through the use of advanced data visualization and data analytics capabilities. It enables organizations to download information in Excel format, allowing them to upload it in their own tracking systems for comprehensive monitoring of all oversight recommendations. In 2026, part of this information will be made publicly available on the Unit’s website, and this increased transparency on the acceptance and implementation of its recommendations for each organization and for each report and note is expected to further improve responsiveness to recommendations.
31.II.11	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 31.II.I).
Figure 31.II.I
Performance measure: Percentage of recommendations issued in the prior eight years for which no acceptance decision has yet been communicated by the organizations to which they were addressed
Planned results for 2027

Result 1: enhanced management of administrative and programmatic issues by organizations of the United Nations system through the implementation of strategic and operational recommendations of the Joint Inspection Unit – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

31.II.12	The Unit’s work contributed to supporting the implementation of its recommendations by the organizations of the United Nations system through more targeted communication on the results of its reviews, both digital and in person, and a review of the Unit’s report format, which strengthened outreach, dissemination and therefore the usefulness and impact of its reports, which met the planned target.
31.II.13	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see table 31.II.1).
Table 31.II.1
Performance measure

Result 2: strengthened impact of Joint Inspection Unit recommendations – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

31.II.14	The Unit’s work contributed to almost a third (32 per cent) of the recommendations issued in 2025 being aimed at improving effectiveness, coherence and harmonization in the United Nations system, which exceeded the planned target of 30 per cent. The target of 20 per cent of recommendations covering topics related to efficiency and dissemination of good practices was not met (actual: 10 per cent) due to a higher-than-expected number of recommendations (58 per cent compared to a target of 50 per cent) falling in the category related to transparency, accountability, coordination, cooperation, control and compliance.
31.II.15	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see table 31.II.2).
Table 31.II.2
Performance measure

Result 3: strengthened system wide knowledge base and informed decision making through evidence-based oversight findings – Proposed programme plan for 2027

31.II.16	The Unit operates in an environment in which both the expectations for oversight and the complexity of organizational reforms continue to grow. However, despite these challenges, ensuring that each review is objective, comprehensive, based on sound evidence and available in a timely manner remains paramount.
Lessons learned and planned change

31.II.17	The lesson for the Unit was that ongoing financial constraints can be a limiting factor with regard to the ability of many organizations to fully engage in the Unit’s review processes. In applying the lesson, and in this context, the Unit will continue to improve its internal working methods, in particular through better use of technology, streamlined data collection approaches and closer attention to the needs and constraints of participating organizations. Building on measures already initiated, the Unit will continue to refine its methodologies to ensure the delivery of a consistent number of timely, impartial and impactful system-wide reports and notes on a wide range of topics, while preserving the analytical depth and practical value expected by stakeholders.
31.II.18	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 31.II.II).
Figure 31.II.II
Performance measure: number of reports and notes issued and percentage of reviews covering all or several participating organizations
Legislative mandates
31.II.19	The list below provides all mandates entrusted to the Joint Inspection Unit.
General Assembly resolutions

text: III.	United Nations System Chief Executives Board for Coordination

children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

31.III.1	CEB, composed of the Secretary-General and the executive heads of the organizations of the United Nations system, is responsible for promoting coherence, cooperation and coordination in the policies, programmes and activities of the organizations of the United Nations system in accordance with their mandates and in response to the decisions of intergovernmental bodies. The mandate of CEB derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly and Economic and Social Council resolutions and decisions, including Council resolution 13 (III) and decision 2001/321. At a time when the international community is confronted by increasingly complex and interrelated global challenges, coordinated and coherent action by the entities of the United Nations system is imperative. Mobilizing the system’s collective capacity is vital to supporting Member States in the implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, the Pact for the Future and the full range of mandates entrusted to United Nations system entities.
Programme of work
Objective
31.III.2	The objective, to which CEB contributes, is to leverage the collective capacity of the organizations of the United Nations system for the effective and efficient delivery of mandates.
Strategy and external factors for 2027
31.III.3	To contribute to the objective, CEB will:
(a)	Provide vision and leadership to guide the effective, coherent and coordinated implementation of mandates by United Nations system organizations;

(b)	Provide a forum for senior leaders of the United Nations system to agree on strategic action, enhance cooperation and pursue policy coherence in both programmatic and administrative areas and coordinate the use of resources, capacities and knowledge;
(c)	Provide a central platform for collective leadership and alignment in relation to reform initiatives, including the UN80 Initiative, within existing mandates and governance frameworks;
(d)	Through its High-level Committee on Programmes:
(i)	Foster strategic system-wide policy coherence and programme coordination in response to intergovernmental mandates and in support of internationally agreed development goals;
(ii)	Develop shared strategies, approaches and frameworks to address emerging policy and programme issues requiring system-wide action;
(e)	Through its High-level Committee on Management:
(i)	Accelerate the development of innovative approaches to improve efficiency and simplify and integrate business operations, in line with best practices;

(ii)	Utilize its functional networks on finance and budget, human resources, digitization and technology, supply chains and safety and security to mainstream innovative management approaches and partnerships and to collect and publish comprehensive system-wide financial and human resources data.
31.III.4	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Progress towards the implementation of the 2030 Agenda by Member States and the full range of mandates entrusted to United Nations system entities, including the follow-up to and implementation of the Pact for the Future and its annexes;
(b)	A more coherent, coordinated and efficient United Nations system response to global challenges;

(c)	Greater capacity of United Nations system organizations to provide effective management responses that drive systemic change in support of the delivery of mandates, through a focus on providing better data analytics; innovation and digital transformation; strategic foresight; and a work culture that encourages stronger results orientation;

(d)	Improved analytical quality of United Nations system-wide comprehensive, comparable financial and human resources data with enhanced transparency and accountability regarding system-wide financial flows.

31.III.5	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the planning assumption that member organizations commit themselves and make timely contributions to the issues addressed by CEB.

31.III.6	CEB integrates a gender perspective in its activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. The CEB annual human resources statistics will continue to systematically track gender representation across the system. The CEB secretariat will continue to track the financial contribution of United Nations activities to gender equality and the empowerment of women through the annual CEB financial statistics. In addition, the CEB secretariat will support the High-level Committee on Programmes in continuing to address gender equality and women’s empowerment across the Committee’s agenda.

31.III.7	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the CEB High-level Committee on Management will continue to accelerate disability inclusion within organizations’ internal operations through the activities of its cross-functional networks.
31.III.8	CEB encourages a coordinated approach to multilingualism across CEB member organizations.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

31.III.9	Recognizing the UN80 Initiative’s goal of “creating a more agile, integrated and effective United Nations system”, the CEB secretariat will support the Secretary-General in his or her capacity as Chair of CEB, to facilitate coordinated action in the context of the initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.
Programme performance in 2025
Far-reaching efficiency initiatives to collectively streamline United Nations system operations, reduce costs and sustain impact

31.III.10	In the context of sustained financial constraints across the United Nations system, coordinated action was undertaken to safeguard mandate delivery and operational integrity. To harness the collective strength of the United Nations system for transformative change, the High-level Committee on Management launched far-reaching efficiency initiatives designed to simplify, integrate and modernize core business operations. In 2025, the Committee rapidly converged on priority areas in relation to procurement and supply chains, finance, human resources, digital foundations and shared services, which demonstrated the potential to reduce fragmentation and cost and to unlock efficiencies at scale. As a dynamic set of solutions, the far-reaching efficiency initiatives help find efficiencies, avoid duplication and ensure that United Nations system organizations stay focused on delivering on their mandates in the most efficient and effective way possible.
31.III.11	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 31.III.1).
Table 31.III.1
Performance measure

table_content.text: Shared efficiency solutions, in areas such as collaborative ICT procurement, treasury services and common insurance services, were initiated to safeguard mandate delivery and operational integrity across the United Nations system

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: United Nations Digital ID, a building block in enabling interoperability across United Nations organizations – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

31.III.12	The CEB secretariat’s work contributed to the Digital ID being available for two additional use cases, which provides a solid foundation for the next phase of high-value and more complex efficiency use cases, as well as being utilized in more United Nations organizations, enabling efficient data transfer and collaboration across United Nations system organizations, which met the planned target.

children.text: Result 3: United Nations system coordination and coherence delivers efficient, sustained and principled action – Proposed programme plan for 2027

31.III.16	The United Nations system is operating in a context of evolving global challenges. In 2025, the CEB secretariat supported the high-level committees of CEB in analysing the related implications for the United Nations system in both the management and the policy and programme areas and in identifying ways to further leverage the capacities of the United Nations system to meet changing needs, complementing and supporting efforts under the Secretary-General’s UN80 Initiative. The CEB secretariat has continued to support various collaborative initiatives subsequently launched under the auspices of CEB to promote efficiency and cost-effectiveness, support coherence and uphold the values and principles of the United Nations in order to support United Nations system entities in delivering results for people.
Lessons learned and planned change

31.III.17	The lesson for the CEB secretariat, based on its experience in 2025, was that in the current context of sustained funding constraints and shifting programmatic priorities, coordination and coherence are essential means through which United Nations system entities can effectively align their efforts and maximize the impact of limited resources. In applying the lesson, the CEB secretariat will support multiple inter-agency workstreams that are expected to advance efficient, sustained and principled action to deliver for the people the United Nations serves, complementing other reform efforts under way in the respective organizations.
